proposals.title: A Systems-Level Map of TLR Signaling Networks in Dendritic Cells Reveals Fibrosis-Promoting Circuits
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling in dendritic cells (DCs) is a cornerstone of immune activation, but its dysregulation is implicated in chronic inflammatory diseases, including fibrosis. Current models are largely linear and fail to capture the complex, context-dependent network behaviors that dictate functional outcomes like pro-fibrotic cytokine production. A systems-level understanding is needed to explain how specific TLR inputs are processed to drive pathological tissue remodeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR signaling in DCs operates as a tunable network with specific regulatory motifs and feedback loops, and that chronic fibrotic stimuli rewire this network to establish a self-sustaining, pro-fibrotic signaling state.
proposals.method: We will construct a logic-based, ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of the core TLR signaling network (MyD88/TRIF pathways, NF-κB, IRFs, MAPKs) in DCs, parameterized with quantitative phospho-proteomic and transcriptomic time-series data from murine bone marrow-derived DCs stimulated with specific TLR ligands (e.g., LPS, CpG). Network inference will be performed using tools like CellNOpt, and model predictions of network rewiring under chronic stimulation will be validated using genetic perturbations (CRISPRi knockdown of key nodes) and cytokine multiplexing.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first predictive computational model of TLR signaling networks in DCs, identifying critical control points and potential 'circuit breakers' for pathological signaling. It will directly bridge Medzhitov's foundational work in TLR biology and immunology with his recent focus on fibrosis, providing a mechanistic framework for how immune sensing drives fibrotic tissue biology.
proposals.novelty: While TLR pathways are well-characterized molecularly, applying a rigorous 'circuit-to-target' systems biology approach—akin to recent work in cardiac fibrosis—to DC signaling networks is novel. This moves beyond descriptive cataloging to predictive modeling of how network dynamics dictate functional immune outputs in fibrosis.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Dissection of a TLR-Responsive Dendritic Cell Circuit Driving Fibrotic Tissue Remodeling
proposals.motivation: Tissue fibrosis is a maladaptive wound-healing response that underlies organ failure, yet the initiating immune signals remain poorly defined. Dendritic cells (DCs) are key sentinels that sense damage via Toll-like receptors (TLRs), but pre-2026 work reveals profound heterogeneity in DC subsets and their downstream effector programs. A precise, circuit-level understanding of how specific TLR-activated DC states instruct fibroblast activation and fibrosis is critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a discrete, TLR-responsive DC subpopulation, identifiable by single-cell transcriptomics, establishes an autocrine signaling circuit that drives a pro-fibrotic tissue microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will employ high-throughput single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) on sorted DCs from TLR-ligand challenged murine tissues. Data will be analyzed using Seurat for clustering, trajectory inference (Monocle3), and cell-cell communication prediction (CellChat). This will be integrated with spatial transcriptomics (Visium) to map DC-fibroblast interactions and validated using genetic DC-subset ablation models.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific DC transcriptional states and paracrine/autocrine loops that initiate fibrosis, moving beyond generic 'inflammation' models. It will establish a new paradigm for tissue biology where immune cell circuits are primary drivers of stromal pathology, identifying novel cellular targets for anti-fibrotic therapies.
proposals.novelty: While DCs are known to respond to TLRs, applying Medzhitov's 'circuit-to-target' framework to dissect their heterogeneity in fibrosis is novel. This approach moves from cataloging cell types to defining the causal logic of a pathological cellular circuit.

proposals.title: Synthetic Control of Toll-like Receptor Signaling to Dissect Fibrotic Reprogramming in Cardiac Stromal Cells
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling is a critical nexus linking innate immune sensing to tissue repair and fibrosis, a maladaptive process central to heart failure. However, the specific contributions of distinct TLR pathways and their spatiotemporal dynamics in activating cardiac fibroblasts into pro-fibrotic myofibroblasts remain poorly defined due to a lack of tools for precise, cell-type-specific pathway control. This gap hinders the development of targeted anti-fibrotic therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic, ligand-gated TLR receptors (synTLRs) can be engineered to deliver precise, user-defined signals to cardiac fibroblasts, and that dissecting these signals will reveal a specific TLR-adaptor circuit that drives autocrine myofibroblast persistence and cardiac fibrosis.
proposals.method: We will employ a synthetic biology approach using a modular receptor architecture. A custom extracellular nanobody will be fused to transmembrane and intracellular domains from specific TLRs (e.g., TLR4) and downstream adaptors (MyD88, TRIF). These synTLRs will be expressed in primary murine cardiac fibroblasts via lentiviral vectors. Their activation will be controlled by a synthetic, inert small-molecule ligand, enabling precise temporal control. We will combine this with single-cell RNA-sequencing and a targeted, longitudinal in vivo imaging system in a mouse model of pressure overload to map the resulting fibrogenic transcriptional programs and tissue-level outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for the causal dissection of innate immune signaling in fibrosis, moving beyond correlative observations. It will directly identify which TLR signaling modules are necessary and sufficient for fibroblast activation and persistence, offering novel, specific targets for intervention. The findings will bridge Medzhitov's themes of immune sensing, tissue biology, and fibrotic circuits.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic receptors are used in immunotherapy, their application to deconstruct fundamental innate immune signaling circuits in stromal cells and fibrosis is novel. This approach moves beyond genetic knockouts or global agonists/antagonists by enabling precise, reversible, and combinatorial signaling dissection within a specific cell type in vivo, aligning with the 'circuit to target' philosophy.

proposals.title: Defining a Minimal Innate Instructive Code: Single-Cell Mapping of TLR Ligand Combinations Sufficient for Naïve T Cell Priming
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor (TLR) signals are critical for initiating adaptive immunity, but the precise combinatorial logic by which dendritic cells integrate these signals to license T cell priming remains poorly defined. This is a fundamental gap in immunology with direct implications for understanding sterile inflammation, fibrosis, and designing next-generation adjuvants. A reductionist, 'circuit-to-target' approach can dissect this essential instructive program.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal, non-redundant set of specific TLR ligand-induced transcriptional modules in dendritic cells is necessary and sufficient to program the transition from naïve to primed T cell state, and that this set is smaller than the full inflammatory repertoire.
proposals.method: We will employ a reductionist in vitro system using bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDCs) and naïve OT-II T cells. Using a defined panel of pure TLR agonists (e.g., Pam3CSK4 (TLR1/2), Poly(I:C) (TLR3), LPS (TLR4), R848 (TLR7/8)), administered singly and in pairwise combinations, we will identify minimal combinations that induce robust T cell proliferation (CFSE dilution) and Th1 polarization (IFN-γ ELISPOT). The critical DC state will be defined by single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics) of the priming DCs, followed by computational deconvolution using network analysis algorithms (e.g., SCENIC) to identify core regulatory gene circuits predictive of T cell priming capacity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational 'minimal signal code' for innate instruction of adaptive immunity. It will provide a precise molecular framework to understand how dysregulated innate sensing in sterile contexts (e.g., tissue damage leading to fibrosis) might inappropriately engage T cells. The findings will offer a new paradigm for adjuvant design based on essential logic rather than maximal inflammation.
proposals.novelty: While TLR roles in immunity are well-known, systematically defining the *minimal sufficient combinatorial signal* for T cell priming is novel. This moves beyond cataloging individual TLR effects to establishing a parsimonious, circuit-based rule set, aligning with Medzhitov's recent focus on tissue biology and circuit-to-target approaches in fibrosis.

proposals.title: Targeted Suppression of TLR4 Signaling in Tissue-Resident Fibroblasts Disrupts a Pro-Fibrotic Autocrine Circuit
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation and fibrosis are central to many autoimmune and degenerative diseases, yet therapeutic strategies often broadly suppress immunity, causing significant side effects. Pre-2026 work, including from Medzhitov's lab, has identified specific autocrine signaling loops, such as those involving IL-11, that drive fibroblast activation and fibrosis. This creates a need for precise interventions that target pathogenic innate immune signaling within specific stromal cells, rather than global immune suppression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Toll-like Receptor 4 (TLR4) signaling within tissue-resident fibroblasts is a critical node sustaining a pro-fibrotic autocrine circuit, and that its cell-type-specific suppression will ameliorate fibrosis without compromising systemic host defense.
proposals.method: We will employ a 'circuit to target' approach, combining advanced methods used in the lab. First, we will use single-cell RNA-sequencing (scRNA-seq) and spatial transcriptomics of fibrotic cardiac tissue from mouse models to map TLR expression and downstream pathway activity specifically in fibroblast subpopulations. Second, we will generate fibroblast-specific TLR4 knockout mice (using Col1a2-CreER) and assess fibrosis in pressure-overload and angiotensin-II infusion models. Third, we will use long-term live-cell imaging and phospho-flow cytometry to dissect the autocrine loop dynamics (e.g., TLR4->NF-κB->IL-11/other mediators) in primary fibroblasts upon targeted TLR4 suppression using a novel, cell-penetrating inhibitory peptide.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel, fibroblast-intrinsic role for TLR4 in sustaining fibrosis, moving beyond its classical role in immune cell activation. It will provide a proof-of-concept for 'circuit-breaking' therapies that target innate immune receptors in a cell-type-restricted manner, offering a more precise strategy for treating fibrotic diseases.
proposals.novelty: While TLR4 agonists are well-studied for immune activation, the therapeutic potential of suppressing TLR signaling in non-hematopoietic stromal cells to treat fibrosis is largely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates Medzhitov's themes of immune sensing, tissue biology, and fibroblast circuits to pivot TLR modulation from immune enhancement to targeted stromal silencing.

proposals.title: A Danger-Context Network Model of Immune-Mediated Fibrosis: Integrating Tissue Stress Signals and Cellular Circuits for Homeostatic Regulation
proposals.motivation: The binary self/non-self paradigm fails to fully explain why immune responses are triggered against some 'self' tissues in fibrosis but not others, or why persistent, low-grade tissue stress leads to maladaptive fibrotic repair. A systems-level understanding of immunity as a homeostatic network, responding to contextual danger signals (DAMPs) rather than foreignness alone, is needed to explain the initiation and chronicity of fibrotic disease. This conceptual shift is critical for developing therapies that correct dysregulated tissue repair without causing immunosuppression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that organ fibrosis is driven by a self-sustaining, maladaptive circuit wherein core immune sentinel cells (e.g., tissue-resident macrophages, stromal cells) interpret persistent Damage-Associated Molecular Patterns (DAMPs) from stressed parenchyma as a continuous 'danger' signal, thereby shifting their regulatory network state from homeostatic tissue surveillance to pro-fibrotic effector programming.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced single-cell multi-omics (scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics) on a well-characterized mouse model of cardiac fibrosis to map the dynamic cellular network. We will computationally model this network using established graph theory algorithms (e.g., centrality analysis, community detection) to identify key 'hub' cell states and signaling pathways that define the homeostatic-to-fibrotic transition. This will be coupled with genetic perturbation (e.g., conditional DAMP receptor knockout in specific stromal and myeloid cells) to functionally validate predicted network hubs and disrupt the proposed autocrine myofibroblast-immune loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, mechanistic framework linking the Danger Model to a dysregulated tissue homeostatic network in fibrosis. It will identify novel, context-dependent immune-stromal circuits that could be targeted to reset network homeostasis. The findings will challenge the field to move beyond pathogen-centric models of immunity toward a systems view of immune function in tissue biology.
proposals.novelty: While the Danger Model exists conceptually, this proposal uniquely operationalizes it as a testable, quantitative network model within a specific disease context (fibrosis), integrating Medzhitov's recent focus on autocrine fibrotic loops and tissue biology with core immunological theory. The application of ecological network stability principles to intra-tissue immune-stromal ecosystems is a non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: Defined Adjuvant Danger Signals Reveal Circuit-Level Failures in Innate Immune Control that Drive Autoimmune Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Adjuvants are critical for vaccine efficacy but can, in susceptible contexts, precipitate autoimmune pathology. The mechanistic link between specific adjuvant signals and the failure of innate regulatory circuits that normally contain inflammation remains poorly defined. This gap limits our ability to predict or therapeutically correct the transition from controlled immune activation to self-sustaining autoimmunity and fibrosis, a key process in diseases like systemic sclerosis and rheumatoid arthritis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, defined adjuvant danger signals (e.g., TLR4 or TLR9 agonists, alum) will reveal non-redundant failure points in innate immune control circuits, and that these failures are both necessary and sufficient to convert a transient adjuvant signal into a persistent, fibrotic autoimmune cascade in genetically susceptible hosts.
proposals.method: We will employ a 'circuit-to-target' approach, treating defined adjuvants as primary experimental inputs in murine models of varying genetic susceptibility (e.g., T-bet deficiency, Sle1 locus). Using longitudinal single-cell RNA-sequencing and spatial transcriptomics of affected tissues (skin, lung), we will map the dynamic recruitment of regulatory cell populations (Tregs, regulatory macrophages) and cytokine feedback loops. Advanced computational methods, including causal network inference (using algorithms like PIDC or SCODE) and trajectory analysis (PAGA, Monocle3), will be applied to identify which specific circuit failures—in regulatory cell recruitment, cytokine thresholds, or antigen-presentation dynamics—are causally linked to the breakdown of tolerance and onset of fibrosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal framework linking specific adjuvant danger signals to the failure of defined innate control circuits, moving beyond correlation. It will identify novel, circuit-based therapeutic targets to prevent the adjuvant-driven breakdown of tolerance. Furthermore, it will provide a paradigm for understanding how environmental triggers interact with genetic susceptibility to initiate fibrotic autoimmune disease.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the adjuvant paradigm from a simple immune 'booster' to a primary experimental tool for deconstructing innate control failure. By integrating the 'circuit-to-target' logic from fibrosis research with causal analysis of autoimmune initiation, it provides a novel, mechanistic roadmap from a defined trigger to a dysregulated pathological state.

proposals.title: Spatial and Functional Deconvolution of the Endogenous Adjuvant Niche Driving Fibrotic Tissue Remodeling
proposals.motivation: Fibrosis is a maladaptive tissue repair process driven by immune-activated fibroblasts. While sterile injury and infection are known triggers, the precise spatiotemporal microenvironments that provide the endogenous adjuvant signals to license this pathological response remain unmapped. A molecular catalog of damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) is insufficient, as immune activation in vivo requires specific combinatorial codes and cellular contexts that are lost in reductionist assays.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sterile injury and localized infection create discrete, transient tissue micro-niches defined by a unique combination of stromal and immune cells, extracellular matrix components, and signaling molecules that collectively function as an endogenous adjuvant, instructing fibrotic reprogramming via spatially coordinated immune-fibroblast crosstalk.
proposals.method: We will use CD11c-YFP-NF-κB and CD11c-YFP-IRF3 reporter mice to identify and isolate, via laser capture microdissection, tissue regions exhibiting adjuvant activity (reporter activation) following cardiac pressure overload or localized S. aureus infection. These niches will be analyzed using the GeoMx Digital Spatial Profiler for whole transcriptome analysis and CODEX (CO-Detection by indEXing) for high-plex protein mapping (50+ targets) to define the complete cellular and molecular milieu. Data integration will use non-negative matrix factorization and spatial correlation algorithms to deconvolute multicellular interaction modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from a list of potential DAMPs to a functional atlas of adjuvant microenvironments, defining the essential combinatorial codes that drive pathological immune activation in fibrosis. It will identify novel cellular hubs and ligand-receptor circuits that could be therapeutically targeted to disrupt the fibrotic cascade without globally suppressing immunity.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying high-resolution spatial 'omics not to cell types, but to dynamic functional states (adjuvant activity) within living tissue, directly testing the concept of tissue-based immune regulation central to Medzhitov's recent paradigm. It shifts the focus from single molecules to the spatial organization of multicellular signaling units.

proposals.title: A Tissue-Level Circuit for Adjuvant Efficacy: Defining the Essential Stromal-Immune Network for T Cell Priming
proposals.motivation: Adjuvants are critical for vaccine efficacy, yet their mechanism is understood primarily through isolated receptor-ligand interactions in immune cells. This ignores the integrated tissue context where priming occurs. A pre-2026 paradigm shift, championed by Medzhitov, views tissues as integrated circuits; applying this to the lymph node is essential to distinguish core from redundant adjuvant-driven pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adjuvant activity requires a non-redundant, tissue-level signaling circuit, wherein specific dendritic cell-stromal cell crosstalk provides essential, integrated costimulatory signals that license antigen presentation and direct T cell fate.
proposals.method: We will use a barcoded library of defined microbial adjuvants (e.g., TLR, NLR, STING agonists) in a mouse model, followed by high-plex spatial transcriptomics (10x Genomics Visium) and CODEX multiplexed proteomics on draining lymph nodes. Cell-cell communication networks will be reconstructed using NicheNet and CellPhoneDB v4.0, and signaling trajectories will be modeled with PAGA and RNA velocity to identify critical checkpoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the minimal sufficient tissue circuit for adjuvant-driven priming, moving the field from a 'molecular checklist' to a 'circuit requirement' model. It will identify novel stromal-immune checkpoints that could be targeted to modulate vaccine responses or treat immunopathology, directly extending Medzhitov's 'circuit' approach from fibrosis to immunology.
proposals.novelty: This is the first application of a circuit-targeting approach, used previously in fibrosis, to deconstruct adjuvant function. It uniquely uses barcoded adjuvant libraries with spatial multi-omics to causally link specific adjuvant inputs to a sufficient tissue-level output program, distinguishing core pathways from context-dependent redundancy.

proposals.title: A Functional Signaling Map of Microbial Adjuvants in the Breakdown of Self-Tolerance
proposals.motivation: The breakdown of self-tolerance is a fundamental event in autoimmune disease, yet the precise molecular logic by which innate immune signals (adjuvants) convert a self-antigen into a target for adaptive immunity remains poorly defined. Current models often use complex, undefined adjuvants, obscuring the specific signaling pathways responsible for redefining antigenic context. A systematic, reductionist approach is needed to link defined adjuvant structures to specific signaling nodes and their functional capacity to breach tolerance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct microbial adjuvants (PAMPs) drive the breakdown of self-tolerance by activating unique, rank-ordered hierarchies of costimulatory signaling pathways in antigen-presenting cells, and that the functional outcome can be predicted by the specific engagement of nodes like MyD88, TRIF, or downstream kinase cascades.
proposals.method: We will employ a reductionist system combining pure model self-antigens (e.g., ovalbumin in RIP-OVA transgenic mice) with isolated, defined PAMPs (e.g., LPS, poly(I:C), CpG, flagellin). Using genetic and pharmacologic tools, we will systematically inhibit specific signaling nodes (MyD88, TRIF, TBK1, RIPK1, MAPKs) in dendritic cells in vivo and in vitro. The breach of tolerance, measured by T cell activation, autoantibody production, and tissue infiltration, serves as the functional readout to create a quantitative map of adjuvant-signaling pathway potency.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a mechanistic 'rulebook' that directly links adjuvant structure to specific signaling modules and their efficacy in breaking self-tolerance. It will move the field beyond correlative associations to establish causal, hierarchical relationships between innate sensing pathways and autoimmune risk, with implications for adjuvant design and understanding disease etiology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using the breach of self-tolerance as a functional screening tool to rank-order the autoimmune-potentiating capacity of specific signaling pathways, fusing precise immunological models with a systematic dissection of adjuvant biology rarely applied to tolerance contexts.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Dectin-1 Clustering Instruct a Fibrosis-Promoting Cytokine Relay in Cardiac Stromal Cells
proposals.motivation: Innate immune responses are central to tissue repair and fibrosis, but the mechanisms that convert microbial detection into pathological fibroblast activation remain unclear. A deterministic view, where a microbe class triggers a fixed response, fails to explain the diverse outcomes of sterile versus infectious injury. This proposal addresses the critical gap in understanding how the physical context of receptor engagement, rather than just ligand identity, programs cytokine networks that drive fibrosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatiotemporal dynamics of a single pattern recognition receptor, Dectin-1, specifically its ligand-induced clustering and spatial segregation from inhibitory co-receptors, determines the activation of distinct signaling modules that program a pro-fibrotic cytokine relay in cardiac stromal cells.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced long-term live-cell imaging using lattice light-sheet microscopy to visualize Dectin-1 clustering dynamics in primary cardiac fibroblasts upon exposure to defined fungal PAMPs (e.g., curdlan). Spatial transcriptomics (MERFISH) and multiplexed cytokine secretion profiling (IsoPlexis) will map the resulting signaling cascades and cytokine outputs. Computational modeling using agent-based simulations will integrate the spatiotemporal receptor data with cytokine networks to define the causal logic of the relay.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for how innate immunity instructs tissue remodeling by linking nanoscale receptor dynamics to organ-scale pathology. It will identify the specific signaling nodes within the Dectin-1 pathway that are druggable targets to uncouple microbial sensing from maladaptive fibrosis, offering a novel therapeutic strategy for conditions like post-infectious myocarditis.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond cataloguing receptor-ligand pairs to mechanistically dissect how receptor nanoscale organization acts as a dynamic code, a concept not yet applied to fibrosis. It uniquely integrates Medzhitov's focus on tissue biology and immune circuits with cutting-edge spatiotemporal analysis to challenge the deterministic dogma of innate signaling.

proposals.title: An Ecological Network Model of Two-Signal Immune Activation in Tissue Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Fibrosis is a maladaptive tissue response driven by immune-activated myofibroblasts, yet the precise regulatory logic governing this activation in complex tissue environments remains unclear. Pre-2026 ecological network theory, used to model species interactions, provides a powerful but untapped framework to understand how immune cells integrate multiple signals (e.g., antigen and danger) to initiate pathological programs like fibrosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that immune cell activation leading to fibrosis operates as an ecological mutualism network, requiring co-occurring 'antigen' and 'danger' signals as non-redundant inputs, and that disrupting this co-dependency can selectively abrogate pathogenic responses while preserving host defense.
proposals.method: We will construct a quantitative ecological network model using Lotka-Volterra-type equations and Boolean logic to define interactions between immune cell 'species' (e.g., T cells, macrophages) and fibroblast 'species' in a simulated tissue niche. The model will be parameterized and validated using pre-2026 single-cell RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics data from fibrotic cardiac and lung tissues. We will employ agent-based modeling (using NetLogo or similar architecture) to simulate network dynamics upon perturbation of either signal axis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical framework for fibrosis, moving beyond linear pathways to a systems-level understanding of immune-stromal crosstalk. It will identify critical network nodes and co-dependency rules that could be targeted to break the autocrine myofibroblast loop, offering new therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: Applying ecological network theory to immune-mediated fibrosis formalizes the two-signal paradigm into a testable, predictive computational model, a significant departure from canonical immunological or purely biochemical descriptions of the process.

proposals.title: A Two-Tiered Adversarial Screen Identifies Functional Receptors for Native-Context Microbial PAMPs in Fibrotic Niche Immune Sensing
proposals.motivation: Fibrosis is increasingly understood as a maladaptive wound-healing response driven by persistent immune activation, often triggered by unresolved microbial cues or tissue damage signals that mimic them. Current receptor discovery relies on purified ligands, which often lack the native structural context and multivalency critical for authentic immune recognition, leading to high rates of false-positive identifications. This gap hinders our understanding of the initial immune triggers that set the stage for chronic inflammation and subsequent fibroblast activation in diseases like cardiac or pulmonary fibrosis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that screening for host receptors using microbial PAMPs presented in their native structural conformation on synthetic membranes will identify a distinct set of high-affinity binders, and that subsequent functional validation via CRISPR-interference will reveal a subset essential for initiating pro-fibrotic immune signaling pathways.
proposals.method: First, we will establish a high-throughput binding screen using a library of candidate immune receptors (e.g., from the IgSF, TLR, or CLR families) expressed on reporter cell surfaces. These cells will be exposed to engineered vesicles displaying purified PAMPs (e.g., bacterial lipopolysaccharide, fungal β-glucans) in their native membrane-embedded, multivalent state. Binding-positive hits will be advanced to a CRISPRi-based functional screen in immortalized macrophage or dendritic cell lines, where knockdown of each candidate receptor will be assayed for specific impairment in NF-κB/IRF activation and cytokine production upon challenge with the same native-context PAMPs.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a shortlist of high-confidence, functionally validated receptors for key microbial PAMPs, directly linking specific pathogen sensing to downstream immune pathways relevant to fibrosis. It will provide a new methodological framework that rigorously couples biophysical binding with cellular function, moving beyond correlative associations. The identified receptors will represent novel potential targets for interrupting the persistent immune signaling that drives pathological fibroblast activation.
proposals.novelty: The adversarial integration of a native-conformation binding screen with a CRISPRi functional screen creates a stringent filter not previously applied to innate immune receptor discovery. This approach directly addresses the critical limitation of non-physiological ligand presentation that plagues classical methods, ensuring discovered receptors are relevant to in vivo immune recognition.

proposals.title: A Control-Theoretic Model of Innate Immune Dynamics Predicts Fibrotic Outcomes from Receptor-Ligand Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation and fibrosis are driven by dysregulated innate immune signaling, yet current therapeutic approaches are largely empirical and fail to predict systemic network effects. Pre-2026 work, including Medzhitov's own on tissue biology and autocrine loops in fibrosis, highlights the need for a quantitative, predictive framework to understand how modulating specific receptor-ligand interactions (e.g., TLRs, NLRs, cytokine receptors) alters tissue-level immune homeostasis and pathological remodeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that innate immune receptor-ligand interactions can be modeled as a multi-input, multi-output control system, and that perturbations to this network, derived from high-throughput pharmacological screening, can predict the stability of the immune response and its propensity to drive fibrotic pathology.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-throughput screening (e.g., using Luminex or transcriptomic readouts in primary macrophages and fibroblasts) to quantify signaling amplitudes and kinetics for a panel of microbial PAMPs and cytokines. These data will parameterize a control-theoretic model built using state-space representations and transfer functions, implemented in MATLAB/Simulink. The model will integrate known feedback loops (e.g., from Medzhitov's 'circuit to target' approach) and will be validated by simulating knock-out/in perturbations of key nodes (e.g., TLR4, IL-1R) and comparing predictions to in vitro co-culture systems and existing murine fibrosis models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, engineering-based framework to predict how pharmacological targeting of innate immune receptors shifts global network stability towards pro-resolution or pro-fibrotic states. It will directly bridge molecular discovery in immunology with predictive outcomes in fibrosis, offering a new paradigm for rational immunomodulator design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit fusion of high-throughput immunological screening with formal engineering control theory to model immune dynamics, moving beyond descriptive network maps to a predictive, stability-analyzing tool for complex tissue pathologies.

proposals.title: Dissecting the cellular circuit of microbial recognition: A stromal-immune relay for epithelial TLR signaling in intestinal fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling in epithelial cells is widely assumed to mediate direct host-microbiota interactions, influencing tissue homeostasis and pathology. However, in fibrosis, a disease of stromal dysregulation, the contribution of non-epithelial cells to these sensing events is unclear. Determining whether epithelial TLR effects are cell-autonomous or relayed through a stromal-immune circuit is critical for understanding the pathogenesis of diseases like intestinal fibrosis and for developing precise therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR ligand recognition in the intestinal epithelium during fibrotic priming is not a direct epithelial event but is instead mediated through a paracrine circuit involving lamina propria stromal cells (LPSCs) and type 2 innate lymphoid cells (ILC2s), which subsequently drive epithelial reprogramming and myofibroblast activation.
proposals.method: We will employ a 'circuit to target' approach using advanced mouse genetics. First, we will use single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) of intestinal tissue from epithelial-specific TLR4 knockout mice and stromal/immune-specific TLR4 knockout mice following fibrotic challenge to map the altered cellular communication network. We will then validate key ligand-receptor pairs using spatial transcriptomics (Visium) and in vitro organoid-LPSC-ILC2 co-culture systems. Finally, we will use inducible, cell-type-specific Cre drivers to sequentially ablate candidate signaling nodes (e.g., IL-13/IL-33) in this circuit in vivo to establish causality.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel cellular relay mechanism for microbial sensing in the gut, shifting the paradigm from direct epithelial recognition to a multicellular circuit. It will identify the specific stromal and immune cell types that are the essential interpreters of TLR signals in a fibrotic context, revealing new targets for anti-fibrotic therapy that spare direct epithelial functions.
proposals.novelty: While TLR function is well-studied, the proposal challenges the cell-autonomy of epithelial TLR signaling in vivo, a foundational concept. It uniquely integrates Medzhitov's 'circuit' logic with fibrosis biology to dissect intercellular communication, moving beyond cataloging cell types to defining their necessary interactions.

proposals.title: Toll-like Receptor Signaling Orchestrates a Tissue-Protective Metabolic Dialogue Between Host and Microbiota
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptors (TLRs) are fundamental to immune sensing, but their role extends beyond pathogen detection to tissue homeostasis and repair. Pre-2026 work from Medzhitov and others highlights TLRs in regulating fibrosis and metabolic circuits, yet the specific small-molecule signals that mediate TLR-dependent communication between host tissues and the commensal microbiota remain poorly defined. Identifying these metabolites is crucial for understanding how immune sensing integrates environmental cues to control tissue biology and prevent maladaptive fibrotic responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR deficiency, particularly in stromal or tissue-resident immune cells, disrupts a conserved metabolic circuit, leading to the loss of specific host- and microbiota-derived small molecules that normally suppress pathological myofibroblast activation and fibrosis.
proposals.method: We will perform untargeted metabolomic profiling (via LC-MS/MS and GC-MS) on serum, stool, and cardiac/intestinal tissue from wild-type and TLR2/4/9-deficient mice under homeostatic conditions and following a pro-fibrotic challenge (e.g., angiotensin II infusion or dextran sulfate sodium exposure). Data will be integrated with 16S rRNA gene sequencing of the gut microbiota. We will use advanced computational methods, including the XCMS platform for peak alignment, MetaboAnalyst 5.0 for statistical analysis (PCA, PLS-DA), and correlation network analysis to link specific metabolite clusters with microbial taxa and fibrotic endpoints, defining TLR-dependent metabolic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a new class of TLR-dependent immunometabolites that act as inter-kingdom signals. It will directly extend Medzhitov's 'circuit to target' framework by identifying the small-molecule components of the autocrine/paracrine loops controlling fibroblast biology. The findings will provide a mechanistic link between innate immune sensing of the microbiota and the metabolic regulation of tissue fibrosis.
proposals.novelty: While TLRs are known to influence metabolism and microbiota, this proposal uniquely seeks to identify the specific metabolite signals that are *dependent* on TLR sensing for their production or regulation, positioning them as essential messengers in a tissue homeostasis circuit, rather than as mere downstream effects of inflammation.

proposals.title: A Circuit-Based Mathematical Model of TLR-Microbiota Crosstalk Driving Fibrotic Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation driven by host-microbe interactions is a fundamental driver of fibrosis in multiple organs, but the dynamic signaling circuits linking Toll-like Receptor (TLR) sensing, microbial ecology, and fibroblast activation remain poorly understood. A predictive computational framework is needed to move beyond descriptive associations and identify critical control points in this pathological network, directly addressing the tissue biology paradigm shift called for in recent work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR-mediated immune sensing of the microbiota establishes a self-reinforcing, multi-tissue circuit involving epithelial alarmins and stromal fibroblasts, which can be mathematically modeled to predict tipping points between homeostatic and fibrotic inflammatory states.
proposals.method: We will develop an ordinary differential equation (ODE) model integrating cell population dynamics (epithelial, fibroblast, immune) and microbial ecology. The model will incorporate specific TLR ligand kinetics (e.g., LPS, flagellin) and autocrine/paracrine signaling loops (e.g., IL-1α, TGF-β) derived from experimental data. We will use parameter estimation and sensitivity analysis (via Latin Hypercube Sampling and Partial Rank Correlation Coefficient) to identify key model parameters, and bifurcation analysis to predict outcomes of TLR modulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, mechanistic model of how host-microbe signaling dynamics drive fibrotic progression, offering testable predictions for therapeutic TLR targeting. It will extend the 'circuit to target' philosophy from cardiac fibrosis to the microbiota-immune-stroma axis, creating a generalizable framework for understanding chronic inflammatory disease.
proposals.novelty: While TLR biology and microbiome dynamics have been studied separately, this proposal uniquely integrates them into a predictive circuit model focused on fibroblast fate, a core Medzhitov lab theme. The application of advanced dynamical systems analysis to this specific biological interface is novel.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Epithelial TLR Signaling Circuits Drive Intestinal Fibrosis in a Human Enteroid Model
proposals.motivation: Intestinal fibrosis is a debilitating complication of Crohn's disease, driven by chronic inflammation and epithelial-immune crosstalk. While TLR signaling in immune cells is well-studied, its role in epithelial cells, particularly in initiating pro-fibrotic circuits, remains poorly defined at a cellular resolution. This gap limits our understanding of how the epithelium transitions from a barrier to a driver of pathological tissue remodeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific intestinal epithelial cell subsets, upon TLR ligand sensing, initiate an autocrine signaling loop that directly promotes a pro-fibrotic transcriptional program, establishing a self-sustaining driver of stromal activation and fibrosis.
proposals.method: We will establish pure human intestinal enteroid cultures and stimulate them with defined microbial TLR ligands (e.g., Pam3CSK4 for TLR1/2, LPS for TLR4). Using droplet-based single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics), we will profile the transcriptional response across epithelial lineages. Data will be analyzed using established algorithms (Seurat for clustering, Monocle3 for pseudotime trajectory analysis) to identify ligand-specific, pro-fibrotic gene modules and transitional cell states. Key candidate mediators (e.g., specific cytokines or growth factors) will be functionally validated using CRISPRi knockdown in enteroids co-cultured with primary human intestinal fibroblasts to assess fibrotic output (α-SMA, collagen deposition).
proposals.contribution: This work will define the precise epithelial TLR signaling circuits that initiate fibrosis, moving beyond correlative associations to causal mechanisms. It will establish a reductionist, human-relevant model to dissect the epithelial-autonomous contribution to tissue fibrosis, identifying novel cell-type-specific targets for anti-fibrotic therapy.
proposals.novelty: This approach applies a 'circuit-to-target' logic, pioneered in cardiac fibrosis, to the intestinal epithelium, leveraging the purity of enteroids to deconvolve epithelial-specific TLR responses at single-cell resolution, which is not possible in vivo or in bulk tissue analyses.

proposals.title: Engineered Probiotic Modulation of Toll-like Receptor Circuits to Attenuate Organ Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Chronic, dysregulated immune signaling through pattern recognition receptors like TLRs is a key driver of pathological fibrosis in organs such as the heart and liver. Current anti-fibrotic therapies are broadly immunosuppressive or target downstream effectors, lacking precision. A 'circuit-targeting' approach, inspired by Medzhitov's framework of tissue biology, is needed to rewire specific immune-stromal communication nodes that sustain fibrotic autocrine loops.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rationally engineered probiotic bacterium, expressing a defined combination of agonistic and antagonistic TLR ligands, can be used as a living therapeutic to rebalance innate immune signaling in fibrotic niches, thereby disrupting the autocrine activation of myofibroblasts and attenuating fibrosis.
proposals.method: We will engineer *E. coli* Nissle 1917 to express tunable surface and secreted TLR ligands (e.g., specific lipidated peptides for TLR2 agonism, modified flagellin for TLR5, and inhibitory oligonucleotides for TLR7/9). Using a microfluidics-based long-term co-culture system of primary murine cardiac fibroblasts and macrophages, we will screen bacterial libraries for profiles that suppress TGF-β and IL-11 production. The lead candidate will be tested in murine models of pressure-overload (TAC) and angiotensin II-induced cardiac fibrosis, with outcomes assessed by histology, bulk and single-cell RNA-seq of sorted stromal and immune cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for 'synthetic immunomodulation' using engineered microbes to correct pathological tissue circuits. It will deliver a proof-of-concept therapeutic agent for fibrosis and provide a foundational map of how combinatorial TLR inputs shape fibroblast fate decisions within a tissue microenvironment.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond using microbes as simple delivery vehicles for single drugs, this inverts the problem by designing a microbe's entire immunogenic profile as the therapeutic signal to reprogram a tissue-scale circuit, directly applying Medzhitov's principles of immune sensing and tissue homeostasis to a synthetic biology intervention.

proposals.title: Systemic Metabolite Priming of Innate Immunity: A Mammalian Analog of Plant Systemic Acquired Resistance
proposals.motivation: Innate immunity is classically viewed as a local, non-specific first line of defense. However, the emerging concept of 'trained immunity' in mammals, where innate immune cells develop enhanced responsiveness via epigenetic reprogramming, suggests a capacity for systemic memory. This proposal seeks to bridge a conceptual gap: while trained immunity is often studied via direct pathogen exposure, it remains unknown whether a localized infection can generate specific, circulating signals that prime distant, unexposed tissues, analogous to the well-defined systemic acquired resistance (SAR) in plants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a localized, non-disseminating infection generates specific circulating danger metabolites or lipid mediators that systemically induce a state of trained immunity in distant myeloid precursors and tissue-resident macrophages, thereby enhancing host defense against a subsequent, heterologous infection.
proposals.method: We will establish a localized subcutaneous S. aureus infection model in mice that does not lead to systemic bacteremia. Using untargeted metabolomics and lipidomics on plasma, we will identify infection-induced circulating factors. Candidate metabolites will be tested in vitro for their capacity to induce epigenetic changes (H3K4me3, H3K27ac via ChIP-seq) and functional priming (enhanced cytokine production) in bone marrow-derived macrophages and hematopoietic stem and progenitor cells (HSPCs). In vivo, adoptive transfer of metabolite-exposed HSPCs or direct metabolite infusion will be followed by a secondary, heterologous challenge (e.g., pulmonary C. albicans) to assess enhanced protection.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a novel endocrine-like axis for innate immune priming, moving beyond direct pathogen sensing. It will identify specific endogenous metabolites that act as systemic 'danger signals,' providing a new mechanistic link between localized infection and global host defense. This could inform strategies to boost innate immunity prophylactically in vulnerable populations.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in directly translating the conceptual framework of plant SAR to mammalian immunology, seeking specific systemic priming signals rather than focusing on local inflammatory mediators or direct pathogen recognition. It challenges the assumption that innate immune training requires direct contact with microbial motifs.

proposals.title: Genetic variation in innate immune sensors dictates population heterogeneity in bystander T-cell priming and herd immunity
proposals.motivation: Classical models of herd immunity assume direct pathogen exposure or vaccination. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence shows that innate immune activation by cytokines, DAMPs, or extracellular vesicles from nearby infections can 'prime' or 'educate' adaptive immunity in a bystander fashion. The population-level epidemiological consequences of this phenomenon, and how it is shaped by host genetics, are completely unknown. This gap limits our understanding of real-world immune landscapes and transmission dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic polymorphisms in innate immune sensors (e.g., TLRs, NLRs, cGAS-STING) set variable thresholds for bystander immune priming by indirect signals, thereby creating a genetically determined mosaic of pre-adapted T-cell immunity in a population that influences pathogen spread and herd immunity thresholds.
proposals.method: We will employ a circuit-to-target approach, defining the autocrine and paracrine signaling loops initiated by model DAMPs (e.g., HMGB1, ATP) and cytokines (e.g., type I IFNs, IL-1β) in primary human dendritic cells from donors genotyped for key sensor polymorphisms. Using advanced long-term in vitro T-cell priming assays and single-cell RNA sequencing, we will map the efficiency of cross-reactive memory T-cell generation. These data will be integrated into computational epidemiological models (using modified SIR frameworks) to simulate how sensor allele frequencies shape population-level immunity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm linking innate immune genetics to population epidemiology through indirect immune education. It will provide a mechanistic framework to explain heterogeneity in infection outcomes and refine models of herd immunity by incorporating bystander priming as a variable. The findings could inform personalized risk assessment and public health strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing Medzhitov's 'circuit-to-target' immunology with population genetics and epidemiology, moving beyond cell-autonomous responses to model how genetic variation in sensory circuits creates collective immune properties at the population scale.

proposals.title: Defining a Minimal Antigen-Presenting Circuit: A Functional In Vivo Replacement Assay for Dendritic Cells in Pathogen-Specific Adaptive Immunity
proposals.motivation: Dendritic cells (DCs) are considered the quintessential professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs) for initiating naive T cell responses. However, the field lacks a rigorous, functional test to determine if other cell types can fully recapitulate the DC program to generate protective, long-lived immunity. This question is central to understanding the fundamental logic of immune sensing and tissue-specific responses, themes central to Medzhitov's work on tissue biology and immune circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, defined non-DC cell type, when provided with the appropriate inflammatory context and antigen access, can execute the complete antigen-presentation program to generate functional, pathogen-specific T cell memory that provides protection equivalent to DC-driven immunity.
proposals.method: We will employ CD11c-DTR and Langerin-DTR mouse models for inducible, systemic DC ablation. Candidate APCs (Ly6C+ monocytes, B cells, or lymph node stromal cells) will be FACS-purified, loaded with pathogen-specific antigen, and adoptively transferred into DC-ablated hosts. Recipients will be challenged with diverse pathogens (e.g., influenza, *Listeria*). T cell priming, differentiation, and establishment of long-lived memory will be tracked using MHC-II tetramers and transcriptional profiling. The definitive assay is a secondary challenge to test for sterilizing or accelerated protection, benchmarked against DC-intact controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive functional assay for antigen-presenting capacity, moving beyond correlative markers to a direct test of immunological protection. It will define the minimal cellular and molecular requirements for generating adaptive immunity, potentially revealing fundamental organizing principles of immune circuits in tissues. The findings could reshape paradigms of APC plasticity and redundancy.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies show other cells can present antigen, this proposal uniquely tests the sufficiency of a single, defined non-DC type to execute the *complete* functional program—from naive T cell priming to establishment of protective memory—in a direct, adversarial replacement assay in vivo.

proposals.title: Network Resilience in Immunity: Inducing Functional Redundancy in Antigen Presentation Beyond Dendritic Cells
proposals.motivation: The central dogma of dendritic cell (DC) necessity for naive T cell priming is a cornerstone of immunology, yet it presents a single point of failure for the adaptive immune system. In tissue contexts like fibrosis, where DC function may be impaired or the cellular landscape altered, understanding potential compensatory pathways is critical. This research addresses a fundamental question of system robustness: whether the immune network, like engineered systems, possesses latent redundancy that can be therapeutically engaged.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific molecular signals, mimicking infection or tissue damage, can reconfigure alternative antigen-presenting cells (APCs) such as tissue-resident macrophages or B cells to fully compensate for the loss of DCs, enabling complete priming of naive T cells and initiation of adaptive immunity.
proposals.method: We will employ a DC-depletion model (e.g., Zbtb46-DTR mice) and a defined antigen challenge. Using high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics, we will profile the molecular state of candidate APC populations in lymphoid and peripheral tissues under various inflammatory conditions (e.g., specific TLR agonist/cytokine cocktails). We will then use advanced in vitro co-culture systems with naive T cell reporters to functionally test the antigen presentation and co-stimulatory capacity of isolated, signal-conditioned non-DC APCs, defining the minimal required 'reconfiguration' signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will systematically map the conditions for functional redundancy in antigen presentation, challenging a rigid hierarchy in immunology. It will identify specific signaling nodes that can be targeted to modulate immune initiation, with potential implications for therapies in fibrotic diseases where DC dysfunction occurs and for boosting immunity in compromised states.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit engineering-inspired framework, treating the immune system as a reconfigurable network and actively seeking to induce, rather than just observe, compensatory pathways. It moves beyond descriptive studies of APC plasticity to a causal test of sufficiency.

proposals.title: A Functional Circuit Map of Dendritic Cell States in Tissue Homeostasis and Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Dendritic cells (DCs) are critical sentinels that translate tissue context into immune instruction, yet their functional diversity is poorly mapped beyond a few canonical subsets. This gap is a major barrier to understanding immune-mediated tissue pathologies like fibrosis, where DCs are implicated but their precise roles are undefined. A systematic, spatially-resolved atlas is needed to move from subset lists to a functional understanding of DCs as integrated components of tissue circuits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DCs exist as a continuum of specialized states defined by integrated transcriptional programs and specific tissue microenvironments, and that pathological fibrosis is driven by the aberrant recruitment or instruction of specific DC states that disrupt local tissue-immune circuits.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-resolution atlas by profiling DCs from multiple organs (lung, heart, skin) in homeostasis and fibrosis models using droplet-based single-cell RNA-seq (10x Genomics) and ATAC-seq. Cell states will be defined using graph-based clustering (Seurat, Scanpy) and trajectory inference (Monocle3, PAGA). Spatial mapping will be achieved with Visium Spatial Transcriptomics and CODEX multiplexed imaging, anchoring defined states to precise niches. Computational ligand-receptor analysis (CellPhoneDB, NicheNet) will infer DC interaction circuits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated, spatially-explicit functional map of DC specialization across tissues, defining their roles as circuit components. It will identify novel DC states and their associated tissue niches, and reveal how specific DC circuits are rewired in fibrotic disease, offering new targets for immune-modulatory therapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a neurobiology-inspired 'circuit mapping' framework to DC biology, moving beyond taxonomy to define functional units integrating molecular state, spatial position, and communication logic. This approach directly tests Medzhitov's paradigm of tissue biology where immune cell function is inseparable from its anatomical and signaling context.

proposals.title: A Functional and Transcriptomic Atlas of Dendritic Cell Specialization in Fibrotic and Allergic Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Dendritic cells (DCs) are critical translators of innate signals into adaptive immune responses, but their functional specialization beyond classical subsets (cDC1, cDC2, pDC) remains poorly defined, particularly in complex tissue contexts like fibrosis and allergy. Pre-2026 work, including Medzhitov's own, highlights tissue-specific circuits and immune sensing of allergens, yet a systematic mapping of the full DC spectrum and its role in translating damage or allergen signals into pathogenic T-cell polarization is lacking. This gap limits our ability to target specific DC functions in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within major DC lineages exist specialized, transcriptionally defined sub-states that are programmed by specific tissue-derived innate signals (e.g., fibrotic DAMPs, food allergens) to drive distinct pathogenic T-helper (Th2, Th17) and fibroblast-activating responses.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered approach: 1) Use high-parameter spectral flow cytometry and index sorting to isolate rare DC populations from murine models of cardiac fibrosis and food allergy. 2) Perform single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics) combined with CITE-seq (cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes) to define transcriptional states and surface protein signatures. 3) Apply novel computational pipelines (e.g., SCENIC for regulatory network inference and CellPhoneDB for ligand-receptor analysis) to identify master regulators and predicted communication circuits. 4) Functionally validate using ex vivo co-cultures of sorted DC subsets with naïve T cells or fibroblasts, measuring cytokine output and activation markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first integrated functional and molecular atlas of DC specialization in fibrotic and allergic settings, moving beyond broad subsets to define disease-relevant sub-states. It will identify key DC-derived signals that instruct pathogenic adaptive immunity and fibroblast activation, providing a roadmap for precise immunomodulatory therapies targeting specific nodes within these circuits.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior cataloging efforts, this proposal directly links deep transcriptional profiling of DCs in disease models to their specific functional output in driving pathological immune and stromal cell responses, applying a 'circuit-to-target' logic inspired by Medzhitov's recent fibrosis work to the innate immune sensor population itself.

proposals.title: A Circuit-Based Atlas of Cytokine Relay Logic in Innate-to-Adaptive Immune Communication
proposals.motivation: The immune response to pathogens is orchestrated by defined sequences of cytokine signals between innate sensing cells and lymphocytes, but these 'relay circuits' remain incompletely mapped for many pathogen classes. This gap limits our ability to predict immune outcomes and rationally intervene in infectious diseases, autoimmunity, and fibrosis, where dysregulated cytokine circuits drive pathology. A systematic, causal understanding of these communication pathways is a fundamental problem in tissue biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that each major pathogen class (e.g., viruses, extracellular bacteria, intracellular bacteria, fungi, helminths) triggers a unique, stereotyped sequence of cytokine production from tissue-resident sentinel cells to specific lymphocyte subsets, forming a deterministic relay circuit that dictates the functional immune response.
proposals.method: We will employ a 'circuit-to-target' approach, combining advanced in vivo models (e.g., cytokine reporter mice, targeted cell ablation) with high-resolution temporal profiling (single-cell RNA-seq with time stamps, multiplexed cytokine imaging) during defined infections. Specific computational algorithms, including causal network inference (e.g., based on transfer entropy or dynamic Bayesian networks) and pseudotime trajectory analysis, will be applied to the longitudinal data to reconstruct the directionality and logic of cytokine relays.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a foundational atlas of innate-adaptive cytokine circuits, moving beyond correlative cytokine lists to causal, temporal maps. It will identify critical control nodes in these pathways, offering new targets for therapeutic modulation in infectious and inflammatory diseases, including fibrotic disorders where similar dysregulated circuits between stromal and immune cells may operate.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine functions are well-studied individually, the systematic, causal deconstruction of their sequential relay logic for all pathogen classes represents a novel integration of immunology with circuit biology principles, directly extending Medzhitov's conceptual framework on tissue biology and immune sensing to a comprehensive, mechanistic map.

proposals.title: Unbiased Functional Mapping of Host Detection Logic for Microbial Viability States
proposals.motivation: The immune system's ability to discriminate between live, replicating microbes and dead or inactivated ones is fundamental for appropriate inflammatory and tissue repair responses. This discrimination is implicated in vaccine adjuvant effects, chronic inflammation, and fibrotic sequelae, yet the host's detection logic remains incompletely defined, often studied through candidate pathways. A systematic, functional map of this capability is needed to understand its role in immune homeostasis and pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the host's differential response to microbial viability is not governed by a single dedicated receptor but emerges from the integration of signals from multiple host factors, including metabolic sensors and immune receptors, whose combined activity is required to detect microbial replication.
proposals.method: We will employ a pooled CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in primary macrophages, challenging them with a diverse panel of live versus genetically or pharmacologically inactivated bacteria (e.g., E. coli, S. aureus) and fungi. Differential host gene essentiality for viability detection will be identified by sequencing-based enrichment analysis. Hits will be validated using high-content imaging for phagocytosis and cytokine secretion, followed by single-cell RNA-sequencing and Seahorse metabolic flux analysis on selected knockout lines to define transcriptional and metabolic circuit alterations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, unbiased catalog of host factors required for sensing microbial viability, moving beyond known pattern recognition receptors. It will establish whether this critical immune function relies on dedicated 'viability sensors' or is an emergent property of immune-metabolic networks, directly informing our understanding of infection resolution and sterile injury responses.
proposals.novelty: This approach is agnostic and functional, contrasting with candidate-gene studies, and integrates phenotypic screening with deep transcriptional and metabolic profiling to define the circuit logic of viability detection, aligning with Medzhitov's interest in tissue biology and immune circuits.

proposals.title: A Cytokine Relay Circuit is a Universal Design Principle for Orchestrating Adaptive Immune Responses
proposals.motivation: Recent work from our lab and others has proposed that adaptive immunity is orchestrated by specific cellular circuits, such as an autocrine myofibroblast loop in fibrosis. A key emerging concept is the 'cytokine relay' principle, where sequential, cell-type-specific cytokine signals direct the progression of a response. However, it is unknown if this principle is a generalizable circuit logic governing all adaptive immunity, from Th2-driven allergy to cytotoxic T cell responses against viruses and tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that diverse adaptive immune responses, irrespective of the initiating antigen or effector class, are governed by a universal design principle of a multi-step, feedforward cytokine relay circuit involving distinct sentinel, relay, and effector cell states.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced, long-term live tissue imaging and single-cell multi-omics (scRNA-seq + ATAC-seq) to deconstruct immune responses in established mouse models of food allergy (Th2), viral infection (CD8+ T cell), and sterile injury with fibrosis. Using computational trajectory inference (PAGA, RNA velocity) and ligand-receptor network analysis (NicheNet), we will map the temporal sequence of cytokine signaling and cellular state transitions. The universality of the relay architecture will be tested by targeted in vivo genetic perturbations (e.g., Cre-inducible cytokine receptor knockouts in specific lineages) to break predicted relay steps and assess circuit failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from studying isolated immune mechanisms toward a unified theory of adaptive immune circuit design. It will establish a foundational framework for predicting immune response outcomes and identify critical, conserved control nodes that could be targeted for novel immunomodulatory therapies across a spectrum of diseases.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine networks are well-studied, the explicit test for a conserved, sequential relay circuit as a universal organizing principle across disparate immune contexts is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of immunology and systems biology, bridging Medzhitov's work on allergy, fibrosis, and tissue biology.

proposals.title: Genetic variation in innate immune circuits defines population-level heterogeneity in adjuvant sensing and vaccine immunogenicity
proposals.motivation: Vaccine efficacy varies significantly across human populations, a disparity not fully explained by socioeconomic factors. Pre-2026 genome-wide association studies (GWAS) have identified polymorphisms in innate immune genes linked to vaccine response, but the causal variants and their specific impact on the immune sensing circuits that drive adaptive immunity remain mechanistically opaque. This gap prevents a rational, stratified approach to adjuvant design, which is critical for global health equity and pandemic preparedness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that common genetic variants in pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) and their signaling adaptors create distinct cell-intrinsic 'adjuvant sensing circuits' that, when engaged by defined microbial adjuvants, program quantitatively and qualitatively divergent adaptive immune responses.
proposals.method: We will employ a circuit-to-target approach, using primary human monocyte-derived dendritic cells (moDCs) from genetically sequenced donors. Cells will be stimulated with defined TLR (e.g., CpG, MPLA) and STING agonists. High-dimensional phospho-flow cytometry and single-cell RNA-sequencing will map variant-dependent signaling dynamics and transcriptional programs. Paired T cell co-culture assays and B cell ELISpot will link these innate circuits to functional outputs: T helper cell polarization and antibody-secreting cell frequency. Machine learning (regularized regression, random forest) will integrate genetic, signaling, and response data to predict adjuvant-variant interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a mechanistic, circuit-based framework linking PRR genetics to adjuvant immunobiology. It will move the field beyond correlative GWAS by defining functional immune endotypes. The resulting predictive models will provide a foundational rationale for developing genetically informed adjuvant strategies to maximize vaccine coverage.
proposals.novelty: While genetic links to vaccine response are known, this proposal uniquely applies Medzhitov's 'circuit-to-target' and 'tissue biology' paradigms—focusing on system-level signaling logic within immune cells—to decode how population-level genetic variation functionally reprograms adjuvant sensing, a non-obvious integration of population genetics with quantitative systems immunology.

proposals.title: A Systems-Level Map of the TLR3-to-NF-κB Signalosome: Defining the Core and Context-Dependent Pathways
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor 3 (TLR3) signaling, triggered by dsRNA, is a critical antiviral defense pathway and a key driver of sterile inflammation implicated in fibrosis and tissue remodeling. While the canonical TRIF-dependent pathway is known, pre-2026 evidence suggests significant cell-type-specific and context-dependent signaling complexity. A complete, high-resolution map of this cascade is essential to understand its precise role in immune sensing, tissue biology, and pathological fibrotic responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the TLR3-to-NF-κB signaling cascade is not a linear pathway but a modular signalosome, where core TRIF-dependent components are supplemented by a network of alternative adaptors and kinases whose engagement dictates the amplitude, duration, and functional outcome of NF-κB activation in different tissue environments.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged, advanced proteomic and functional genomics approach. First, we will use BioID proximity labeling (TurboID) fused to TLR3 and TRIF in primary macrophages and fibroblasts to capture the dynamic interactome. Second, we will perform a focused CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen targeting all known and predicted signaling adaptors/kinases, using NF-κB nuclear translocation (live-cell imaging) and specific cytokine output as readouts. Third, we will validate key interactions and their functional necessity using co-immunoprecipitation and reconstitution assays in genetically null cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, experimentally validated wiring diagram of the TLR3-induced NF-κB signalosome. It will identify novel regulatory nodes that could be targeted to modulate TLR3-driven inflammation. Furthermore, by comparing signaling in immune cells (macrophages) and stromal cells (fibroblasts), it will directly inform Medzhitov's interest in how immune sensing pathways dictate tissue biology and fibrotic outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While the core TLR3 pathway is described, this proposal seeks to define the *complete* and *context-dependent* network, moving beyond a canonical view to uncover cell-type-specific modules that may explain differential roles in infection, tissue repair, and fibrosis, integrating Medzhitov's focus on tissue-level biology with deep molecular mechanism.

proposals.title: Mapping the Tissue-Specific TLR3-dsRNA Circuitry: A Determinant of Fibrotic Fate in Stromal and Immune Cells
proposals.motivation: Fibrosis is a maladaptive tissue repair process driven by myofibroblasts, often with immune cell involvement. Pre-2026 work, including from Medzhitov's lab, highlights the importance of autocrine signaling circuits and tissue biology paradigms in fibrosis. While TLR3 is known to sense dsRNA from viruses and damaged cells, its expression and functional role in non-immune stromal cells (e.g., fibroblasts, epithelial cells) across different tissues remain poorly characterized, creating a gap in understanding how innate immune sensing directly shapes fibrotic microenvironments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR3 expression and responsiveness to endogenous dsRNA is a cell-type and tissue-specific feature that establishes a pro-fibrotic circuit in resident stromal cells, which is modulated by adjacent immune cells to either amplify or resolve fibrotic signaling.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and cellular indexing of transcriptomes and epitopes (CITE-seq) on primary cells from murine and human fibrotic (lung, liver, heart) and healthy tissues to map TLR3 expression at high resolution. Using ex vivo co-culture systems of sorted cell populations (e.g., fibroblasts, macrophages, epithelial cells), we will challenge them with synthetic dsRNA (poly(I:C)) and endogenous dsRNA released from necrotic cells. Functional readouts will include phospho-flow cytometry for IRF3/NF-κB signaling, multiplex cytokine profiling, and quantitative imaging of α-SMA and collagen to define the autocrine/paracrine output of the TLR3 circuit.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a comprehensive atlas of functional TLR3 expression across tissue-resident cells, defining which cell types are primary dsRNA sensors in fibrotic niches. It will elucidate a novel innate immune mechanism that drives fibroblast activation independently of classical immune cells, directly linking tissue damage sensing to fibrotic fate. This could identify TLR3 or its downstream effectors as tissue-specific therapeutic targets.
proposals.novelty: While TLR3 is studied in immune cells and some epithelial lines, a systematic, functional mapping in primary stromal cells across tissues is lacking. Integrating this with a 'circuit-to-target' approach to define the autocrine pro-fibrotic loop is a novel application of Medzhitov's conceptual framework to innate immune-driven fibrosis.

proposals.title: TLR3-Dependent Sensing of Viral dsRNA Orchestrates Antiviral Immunity and Fibrotic Pathology in the Lung
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor 3 (TLR3) is a key sensor of viral double-stranded RNA, but its precise role in coordinating antiviral defense versus immunopathology in whole organisms remains incompletely defined. This is critical because excessive or dysregulated immune responses to respiratory viruses can drive chronic tissue remodeling and fibrosis, a major cause of long-term morbidity. Understanding the specific cellular circuits activated by TLR3 in vivo is essential for developing targeted immunomodulatory therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR3 signaling during acute viral pneumonia is essential for early antiviral defense but also initiates a maladaptive immune circuit that promotes pro-fibrotic macrophage and fibroblast activation, leading to persistent tissue dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will employ a circuit-to-target approach, using TLR3-deficient mice and cell-type-specific reporter lines (e.g., LysM-Cre for myeloid cells, Col1a1-Cre for fibroblasts) infected with a controlled dose of influenza A virus. Advanced methods include longitudinal in vivo imaging, single-cell RNA sequencing of sorted lung immune and stromal cells at critical time points, and spatial transcriptomics to map pro-fibrotic signaling niches. Autocrine loops will be defined by co-culture assays of primary alveolar macrophages and lung fibroblasts from these mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the precise immunofibrotic circuit initiated by viral sensing via TLR3, linking acute infection to chronic tissue pathology. It will identify the key cellular players and mediators that could be targeted to uncouple protective immunity from harmful fibrosis. The findings will advance the paradigm of tissue biology by elucidating how innate immune sensing directly reprograms tissue-resident cell states.
proposals.novelty: While TLR3's role in antiviral cytokine production is known, its specific function in orchestrating a pro-fibrotic tissue circuit in vivo, connecting immune sensing to fibroblast fate, is unexplored. This moves beyond cataloging gene expression to defining the causal cellular interactions that drive pathology.

proposals.title: Integrated PRR Circuitry in Fibroblasts: TLR3-Dependent Modulation of RIG-I and NLRP3 Signaling Drives Profibrotic Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Pattern recognition receptor (PRR) crosstalk is a critical, unresolved layer of immune regulation with direct implications for chronic inflammatory diseases like fibrosis. Pre-2026 work, including Medzhitov's focus on tissue circuits and autocrine loops in fibrosis, highlights the need to move beyond single-pathway studies. Defining how TLR3 (sensing dsRNA) integrates with cytosolic RIG-I-like receptors (RLRs) and Nod-like receptors (NLRs) during viral or tissue injury could reveal novel drivers of pathological fibroblast activation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TLR3 signaling in tissue-resident fibroblasts establishes a primed state that selectively amplifies RLR-driven type I interferon responses and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, creating an integrated pro-inflammatory and pro-fibrotic circuit.
proposals.method: We will employ a 'circuit to target' approach using primary murine and human fibroblasts. Specific methods include: 1) CRISPR/Cas9 knockout and siRNA knockdown of specific PRRs and adaptors (TRIF, MAVS, ASC) to dissect pathway hierarchy; 2) Advanced long-term live-cell imaging with FRET biosensors (e.g., for caspase-1 activity) and single-cell RNA sequencing to map dynamic signaling trajectories after sequential or simultaneous ligand challenge (e.g., poly(I:C) for TLR3/RLR, nigericin for NLRP3); 3) Spatial transcriptomics on fibrotic tissue sections to validate co-localization of pathway signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a new autocrine-paracrine circuit where PRR crosstalk in stromal cells dictates the quality of tissue inflammation. It will identify specific nodal points for therapeutic intervention in fibrotic diseases and advance the paradigm of tissue biology as governed by integrated immune-sensory networks.
proposals.novelty: While PRR synergy is known in myeloid cells, its function in fibroblasts—key effectors in fibrosis—and the specific role of TLR3 as a modulator of cytosolic sensor circuits remain largely unexplored, moving beyond cataloging interactions to defining a causal, targetable signaling hierarchy.

proposals.title: Defining the TLR3-Induced Transcriptional Circuitry in Fibroblasts: A Genomic-Scale Map of Innate Immune-Driven Fibrosis
proposals.motivation: Toll-like receptor 3 (TLR3) senses double-stranded RNA, a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) released from necrotic cells in injured tissues. Pre-2026 work, including from Medzhitov's lab, implicates TLR3 in sterile inflammation and fibrosis in organs like the heart and lung.
